The surgeon places trocars under twelve millimeter pressure, ensuring no bowel loops are obstructed. The cecum and ileum are pushed aside, and the appendix is inspected, noting the patient has had an appendectomy. The bowel is examined for solitary nodules of endometriosis that could cause occlusion post-surgery.

The surgeon examines the adnexa and Douglas pouch, noting the patient's previous caesarean section. The uterus is positioned laterally and in an intermediate position to expose the round ligament. Using bipolar scissors or the Thunderbeat device, the surgeon coagulates and incises the round ligament, then manipulates it to access the anterior leaf of the broad ligament, which is incised towards the isthmus.

The surgeon instructs the assistant to push the uterus and valve into the vagina to expose the vaginal cul-de-sac. The peritoneum of the bladder is incised, and vessels are coagulated individually. A window is created in the broad ligament above the ureter, ensuring the ureter remains below. The surgeon decides to keep the ovaries and removes the fallopian tubes by coagulating and cutting at their limit to reduce ovarian cancer risk. The ovary is held while coagulation and cutting are performed, ensuring minimal tension for effective coagulation using bipolar energy.

The surgeon inspects the surgical field to ensure there is no bleeding. They proceed to dissect the posterior leaf of the broad ligament, pushing the ureter laterally to avoid injury. The dissection is directed toward the root of the uterosacral ligament. The ureter is identified and protected while coagulating the uterine vessels at the isthmus. The round ligament is incised and coagulated on the opposite side, with the uterus in an intermediate position. The vaginal cul-de-sac is exposed by pushing the valve, and the anterior leaf of the broad ligament is incised toward the isthmus. The surgeon holds the fallopian tube, noting its interruption due to previous tubal ligation.

The surgeon checks for perfect hemostasis and pushes the valve laterally to safely displace the ureter during dissection. The uterus is antiverted to work on the posterior leaf towards the uterosacral ligament. Large veins are visible, and the assistant holds the round ligament while the surgeon uses bipolar forceps, ensuring the ureter is safely pushed away. The surgeon waits for the disappearance of small bubbles, indicating complete tissue coagulation, before proceeding to cut.

The surgeon identifies the uterine artery and uses a bipolar device to achieve hemostasis. The round ligament is held while the assistant pushes the valve laterally to expose the ureter. Bleeding is observed near the ureter, likely from an opened vein. The surgeon confirms the ureter's location and observes its movement to ensure it is not compromised.

The surgeon controls the uterine pedicles at the isthmus, cutting them to devascularize the uterus, which changes color due to reduced blood flow. Maria pushes the valve to expose the bladder, allowing the surgeon to dissect and incise the cervix and vagina while maintaining contact with the cervix.

The surgeon uses a monopolar hook or energy device to create an incision in the vagina, maintaining close contact with the cervix. The third operator turns the valve to follow the incision, ensuring the round ligament is held securely. The surgeon cautions against excessive pushing to avoid breaking the vagina and emphasizes maintaining distance from the ureter, which is pushed laterally.

The surgeon completes the final centimeters of the procedure, ensuring not to exert excessive pressure on the uterus. The assistant gently removes the uterus, and the surgical instruments are repositioned. The surgeon demonstrates suturing technique, holding the needle between the anterior and posterior thirds, and inserting it perpendicularly through the tissue to ensure proper closure.

The surgeon uses a needle holder to perform simple stitches with Vicryl, ensuring the needle is sized perpendicularly and rotated without translation to avoid tissue turning. The edges are controlled first, followed by additional stitches. The surgeon performs ovariopexy by suturing the ovaries, using pure rotation to pick into the ovary on the antemysial edge and tying two or three knots.

The surgeon adjusts the needle to achieve a perpendicular orientation on the tissue, ensuring precise stitching. The needle is rotated carefully to maintain alignment, and multiple stitches are placed to secure the tissue edges.

The surgeon uses a needle holder to place stitches in the tissue, ensuring the needle is perpendicular to the tissue for effective suturing. The thread is held at a distance from the needle to maintain tension, and the instrument is used to protect the tissue from traction. Hemostasis is confirmed, and additional stitches are placed in the middle of the surgical site.
